1: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα μὴ σκανδαλισθῆτε — "these things I have spoken to you so that you may not stumble." The perfect lelalēka — completed, standing disclosure. Skandalísthēte (G4624, aorist passive subjunctive of skandalízō) — to stumble, to fall into a trap, to be made to fall. The word skandalon (G4625) is a snare or stumbling-block; here the stumbling is apostasy under pressure. The Farewell Discourse as a whole — the promise of the Paraclete, the vine and branches, the friendship promise — is given with this explicit protective purpose.
2: ἀποσυναγώγους ποιήσουσιν ὑμᾶς — "they will make you expelled from the synagogue." Aposynágōgos (G656) — a rare compound occurring only three times in the NT, all in John (John 9:22; John 12:42; John 16:2). This was formal exclusion from Jewish communal life — loss of legal standing, social network, family ties, and economic participation. The term reflects the painful reality of Jewish believers being expelled from synagogues in the late first century. ἀλλ᾽ ἔρχεται ὥρα ἵνα πᾶς ὁ ἀποκτείνας ὑμᾶς δόξῃ λατρείαν προσφέρειν τῷ θεῷ — "but an hour is coming when everyone who kills you will think he is offering service to God." Latreían prospherein — "offering service/worship" — a cultic term used for religious devotion. The persecution of Jesus's followers is framed as religiously motivated violence; the persecutors believe they are acting for God.
3: καὶ ταῦτα ποιήσουσιν ὅτι οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τὸν πατέρα οὐδὲ ἐμέ — "and they will do these things because they have not known the Father or me." The aorist egnōsan — "they did not come to know." The not-knowing is the theological root of the violence. The diagnostic function of Christ's coming (John 15:22–24) is here applied to the persecutors: they do not know the God in whose name they act.
4: ἀλλὰ ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ὅταν ἔλθῃ ἡ ὥρα αὐτῶν μνημονεύητε αὐτῶν — "but these things I have spoken to you so that when their hour comes, you may remember them." The prophetic-predictive function of the Farewell Discourse is made explicit: the disciples will recall these warnings when the persecutions begin. Mnēmoneuēte (G3421, present subjunctive) — keep remembering. ὅτι ἐγὼ εἶπον ὑμῖν — "that I told you." The recollection is of Jesus's own speech — the Evangelist presents the disciples as post-resurrection readers connecting their experience to these words.
5: νῦν δὲ ὑπάγω πρὸς τὸν πέμψαντά με — "but now I am going to the one who sent me." Hypágō (G5217, present indicative) — Jesus is going, already in motion. καὶ οὐδεὶς ἐξ ὑμῶν ἐρωτᾷ με ποῦ ὑπάγεις — "and none of you asks me, 'Where are you going?'" This seems to contradict John 13:36 (Peter's question) and John 14:5 (Thomas's question), both of which are questions about the destination. The Evangelist may mean that no one is now pressing the question with genuine concern for Jesus's destination — grief has displaced inquiry. The disciples have turned inward to their own sorrow rather than outward to Jesus's mission.
6: ἀλλ᾽ ὅτι ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν, ἡ λύπη πεπλήρωκεν ὑμῶν τὴν καρδίαν — "but because I have spoken these things to you, grief has filled your heart." Two perfects: lelalēka (I have spoken, it stands spoken) and peplērōken (grief has filled, and it stands filled). The filling of the heart with grief mirrors the promise that joy will be "completed" (plēroō, v24) — the same verb for both states. The disciples are at the stage of grief-saturation before the Spirit's arrival reverses it.
7: ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ τὴν ἀλήθειαν λέγω ὑμῖν — "but I tell you the truth." The alētheia formula grounds what follows as genuine disclosure, not comfort fabricated to ease their distress. συμφέρει ὑμῖν ἵνα ἐγὼ ἀπέλθω — "it is to your advantage that I go away." Symphérei (G4851, impersonal present of symphérō) — it is profitable, expedient, beneficial. The word is commercial in register: the departure of Jesus produces a net gain. The basis: ἐὰν γὰρ μὴ ἀπέλθω, ὁ παράκλητος οὐ μὴ ἔλθῃ πρὸς ὑμᾶς — "for if I do not go away, the Advocate will not come to you." The double negative ou mē with the subjunctive is the strongest Greek denial: absolutely will not come. The physical presence of the incarnate Jesus and the universal presence of the Spirit are mutually exclusive states; the departure is the condition of the gift.
8: ἐλέγξει τὸν κόσμον περὶ ἁμαρτίας καὶ περὶ δικαιοσύνης καὶ περὶ κρίσεως — "he will convict the world concerning sin and concerning righteousness and concerning judgment." Eléngchō (G1651) — a forensic verb spanning "to expose," "to convict," "to prove guilty," "to convince." In legal usage it means to bring evidence that establishes guilt; the outcome may be conviction leading to repentance or shame. The Spirit performs this in the world as a whole — the kosmos stands in a courtroom, and the Spirit is the prosecuting attorney who brings light on three counts: sin, righteousness, judgment. Each is unpacked in vv9–11.
9: περὶ ἁμαρτίας μέν, ὅτι οὐ πιστεύουσιν εἰς ἐμέ — "concerning sin, because they do not believe in me." The world is convicted of sin not for a catalogue of moral failures but for the foundational sin: unbelief in Jesus. Hamartía (G266) — sin as a condition, not merely individual acts; the world's fundamental orientation away from God. The present tense pisteuousin — "they are not believing" — marks it as an ongoing state. The singular sin that the Spirit convicts is the rejection of the one who revealed the Father.
10: περὶ δικαιοσύνης δέ, ὅτι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα ὑπάγω — "concerning righteousness, because I am going to the Father." Dikaiosýnē (G1343, dispute_level 4) — righteousness, justification, rectitude. The Spirit's convicting work here is vindicating: Jesus's departure to the Father is itself the demonstration of righteousness. The world condemned Jesus as a blasphemer and executed him; the ascension reveals that he was righteous, vindicated by the Father who received him. The Spirit reproves the world's verdict. καὶ οὐκέτι θεωρεῖτέ με — "and you see me no longer" — the disciples' loss of direct sight of Jesus is connected to the world's being reproved: the Spirit continues the work of making Jesus's righteousness present even in his absence.
11: περὶ δὲ κρίσεως, ὅτι ὁ ἄρχων τοῦ κόσμου τούτου κέκριται — "concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world has been judged." Kékritai — perfect passive: "has been judged and it stands judged." The ruler's judgment is already accomplished; the cross is the decisive verdict. Árchōn (G758, ruler) — the same title as in John 12:31 ("now the ruler of this world will be cast out") and John 14:30. The Spirit convicts the world of judgment not by announcing a future event but by exposing that judgment has already occurred: the prince's authority has been broken at the cross.
12: ἔτι πολλὰ ἔχω ὑμῖν λέγειν, ἀλλ᾽ οὐ δύνασθε βαστάζειν ἄρτι — "I have many more things to say to you, but you cannot bear them now." Bastázō (G941) — to carry, to bear a weight. The disciples' incapacity is temporary and situational — "now" (arti) — not permanent. The incompleteness of Jesus's teaching is presented not as a defect but as a pedagogical limit awaiting the Spirit's coming. This verse is the hinge between the Paraclete's three-pronged work in the world (vv8–11) and his revelatory work in the community (vv13–15).
13:
ὅταν δὲ ἔλθῃ ἐκεῖνος, τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς ἀληθείας, ὁδηγήσει ὑμᾶς εἰς τὴν ἀλήθειαν πᾶσαν — "when that one comes, the Spirit of truth, he will guide you into all truth." Hodēgéō (G3594) — to guide, to lead the way, as a guide leads travelers on a road. The Spirit is not a lecturer but a guide who accompanies the journey into truth. Eis tēn alētheian pāsan — "into all the truth" — the scope is comprehensive; what Jesus could not yet say (v12) the Spirit will lead them into. This verse grounds the apostolic teaching of the NT as Spirit-guided revelation.

οὐ γὰρ λαλήσει ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ — "for he will not speak from himself." The Spirit's dependence on the Son mirrors the Son's dependence on the Father (John 14:10). The pattern of the missions is consistent: the Father speaks through the Son; the Son speaks through the Spirit. ἀλλ᾽ ὅσα ἀκούσει λαλήσει — "but what he hears he will speak." The Spirit's speaking is reception before it is transmission — he hears from the Son and speaks to the community.
14: ἐκεῖνος ἐμὲ δοξάσει, ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ ἐμοῦ λήμψεται — "that one will glorify me, because from what is mine he will receive." The Spirit's ultimate purpose is to glorify Jesus — not to be an independent revelatory center but to make Jesus's person and work known. Ek tou emou — "from what is mine" — the Spirit receives from the treasury of the Son. Lēmpsetai (G2983, future of lambánō) — will receive. καὶ ἀναγγελεῖ ὑμῖν — "and will announce/declare to you." Anangéllō (G312) — to report, to declare in detail. The Spirit's announcing of what is Christ's constitutes his ministry of glorification.
15: πάντα ὅσα ἔχει ὁ πατὴρ ἐμά ἐστιν — "all that the Father has is mine." The claim is total: no division of possession between Father and Son. This is the ground for the Spirit's receiving "from what is mine" (v14): the Son's possessions include everything the Father has. διὰ τοῦτο εἶπον ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ ἐμοῦ λαμβάνει — "for this reason I said that he receives from what is mine." The repetition of v14's formula grounds the Spirit's revelatory role in the ontological communion of Father and Son: what belongs to the Father belongs to the Son; the Spirit draws from that shared plenitude.
16: μικρὸν καὶ οὐκέτι θεωρεῖτέ με, καὶ πάλιν μικρὸν καὶ ὄψεσθέ με — "a little while and you will no longer see me, and again a little while and you will see me." Two mikrón (G3397, little while) constructions bracket the period of Jesus's absence. Two seeing-verbs: theōréō (G2334, perceptual, sustained seeing — the disciples' present sight) and horáō (G3708, to see, often of revelatory seeing). After the first little while, the perceptual seeing ends; after the second, the seeing of horáō begins — perhaps the resurrection appearances, or the eschatological vision, or the Spirit-mediated knowing of John 14:19–20. The Evangelist leaves the referent deliberately multi-dimensional.
17: Some of the disciples said to one another — the Evangelist does not name them — "What is this that he says to us, a little while and you will not see me, and again a little while and you will see me?" The repetition of Jesus's exact words signals confusion, not hostile rejection. The disciples are bewildered, not resistant. The Evangelist's technique here is consistent with the pattern of misunderstanding that runs through the Gospel (John 3:4; John 4:11; John 6:42; John 11:12) — misunderstanding that creates the occasion for fuller disclosure.
18: τί ἐστιν τοῦτο ὃ λέγει, τὸ μικρόν — "what is this little while he speaks of?" The disciples focus on the phrase to mikrón — the "little while" has become a puzzle. οὐκ οἴδαμεν τί λαλεῖ — "we do not know what he is saying." The combination ouk oidamen — "we have not come to know" (perfect) — and the present laleí — "is saying" — expresses honest incomprehension of an ongoing utterance.
19: Jesus perceives their desire to ask — egnō (aorist of ginōskō, G1097, the Evangelist's verb for Jesus's supernatural perception of human thought) — and addresses the confusion directly. The rhetorical question περὶ τούτου ζητεῖτε μετ᾽ ἀλλήλων — "are you asking one another about this?" — is not accusatory but invitational: bring the question to me. The disciples' intramural discussion is redirected to direct inquiry of Jesus.
20: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι κλαύσετε καὶ θρηνήσετε ὑμεῖς — the solemn double-amēn introduces the birth-pain analogy. Klaíō (G2799) — to weep, the verb used for Mary's weeping at the tomb (John 20:11) and for the raising of Lazarus (John 11:31). Thrēnéō (G2354) — to mourn loudly, to lament. Both futures describe what the disciples will do. ὁ δὲ κόσμος χαρήσεται — "but the world will rejoice." The world's rejoicing at the crucifixion is a horrifying reversal of the expected order. ἡ λύπη ὑμῶν εἰς χαρὰν γενήσεται — "your grief will become joy." Genēsetai (future of ginomai) — will become, will turn into. The grief does not merely produce joy as a secondary effect; it is itself transformed.
21: ἡ γυνὴ ὅταν τίκτῃ λύπην ἔχει — "the woman when she gives birth has grief." Tíktō (G5088) — to give birth, to bring forth. The analogy is precise: the grief of labor is real and intense (hē hōra autēs — "her hour"), but the birth itself annihilates its memory: οὐκέτι μνημονεύει τῆς θλίψεως διὰ τὴν χαρὰν ὅτι ἐγεννήθη ἄνθρωπος εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "she remembers no more the anguish because of the joy that a man was born into the world." Thrípsis (G2347) — pressure, anguish, tribulation. Egerennēthē (aorist passive) — was born: the new life is not the mother's achievement but a happening that comes upon the world.
22: καὶ ὑμεῖς οὖν νῦν μὲν λύπην ἔχετε — "and you therefore now indeed have grief." The grief is present-tense and real. πάλιν δὲ ὄψομαι ὑμᾶς — "but I will see you again." The future opsomai (G3708) — the same word used in v16 for the disciples' seeing; here Jesus uses it for his own seeing of them. The reunion is mutual seeing. καὶ χαρήσεται ὑμῶν ἡ καρδία, καὶ τὴν χαρὰν ὑμῶν οὐδεὶς αἴρει ἀφ᾽ ὑμῶν — "and your heart will rejoice, and no one takes your joy from you." Aírō (G142) — to take away — the same verb as in John 15:2 (removing branches). The permanence of the resurrection joy is absolute: it is beyond the reach of any human or cosmic removal.
23: καὶ ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐμὲ οὐκ ἐρωτήσετε οὐδέν — "and in that day you will ask me nothing." The question-verb here is erōtáō (G2065) — to inquire, to ask directly in conversation, as one asks a teacher. ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν, ὅ τι ἂν αἰτήσητε τὸν πατέρα ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου δώσει ὑμῖν — the prayer-verb shifts to aitéō (G154) — the asking of petition, of request. The distinction: after the resurrection, the disciples will not need to ask Jesus conversational questions (because they will understand); their asking will be petitionary prayer to the Father in Jesus's name, which will be answered.
24: ἕως ἄρτι οὐκ ᾐτήσατε οὐδὲν ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου — "until now you have asked nothing in my name." The aorist ētēsate — a summary of the entire period of the disciples' pre-resurrection existence. Prayer "in the name of Jesus" is post-resurrection practice; it requires the revelation of who Jesus is that only the resurrection fully provides. αἰτεῖτε καὶ λήμψεσθε — "ask and you will receive." Present imperative + future indicative: the command to ask and the certainty of reception. ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν ᾖ πεπληρωμένη — "so that your joy may be fulfilled." Peplērōmenē (G4137, perfect passive participle) — a completed fullness of joy as the goal of answered prayer.
25: ταῦτα ἐν παροιμίαις λελάληκα ὑμῖν — "these things in figures of speech I have spoken to you." Paroiimía (G3942) — a proverb, a dark saying, an indirect or figurative speech form. The entire vine discourse (John 15) and the "little while" language are examples of paroimía. ἔρχεται ὥρα ὅτε οὐκέτι ἐν παροιμίαις λαλήσω ὑμῖν — "an hour is coming when I will no longer speak to you in figures." The promised plainness (parrhēsía) replaces the figurative mode — a promise fulfilled in the post-resurrection conversations (John 20:19–23; Acts 1:3) and in the Spirit's guiding into all truth (v13).
26: ἐν ἐκείνῃ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματί μου αἰτήσεσθε — "in that day you will ask in my name." The future aitēsesthe — the prayer practice of the resurrection community. καὶ οὐ λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγὼ ἐρωτήσω τὸν πατέρα περὶ ὑμῶν — "and I do not say that I will ask the Father on your behalf." The present tense legō — Jesus is explicitly clarifying what is not meant. This correction pre-empts the inference that the disciples need an additional intercessor to overcome a reluctant Father; v27 supplies the reason: the Father himself already loves them directly.
27: αὐτὸς γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ φιλεῖ ὑμᾶς — "for the Father himself loves you." Autós — emphatic "himself" — the Father personally and directly loves the disciples. The verb here is philéō (G5368) — the love of personal affection, friendship — as opposed to agapáō. The Father's love is not mediated through an additional intercessor; he is already disposed toward the disciples in direct personal love. ὅτι ὑμεῖς ἐμὲ φεφιλήκατε καὶ πεπιστεύκατε ὅτι ἐγὼ παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθον — "because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God." Both verbs are perfect: pephilēkate (you have loved and it stands) and pepisteukate (you have believed and it stands). The disciples' faith and love are completed realities that ground the Father's direct love for them.
28: ἐξῆλθον παρὰ τοῦ πατρὸς καὶ ἐλήλυθα εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "I came out from the Father and have come into the world." Two verbs: exēlthon (aorist — the departure from the Father) and elēlytha (perfect — I have come and am here). The perfect carries the incarnation as a completed act whose result is still present: the Son is here, in the world, having come from the Father. πάλιν ἀφίημι τὸν κόσμον καὶ πορεύομαι πρὸς τὸν πατέρα — "again I leave the world and go to the Father." Aphíēmi (G863) — to release, to let go, to leave behind. The entire movement of the incarnation is summarized: out from the Father → into the world → leaving the world → to the Father. The same arc appears in 13:3 and John 17:5.
29: The disciples' response — ἴδε νῦν ἐν παρρησίᾳ λαλεῖς — "see, now you are speaking plainly" — uses parrhēsía (G3954), the promised mode of speaking from v25. The disciples believe the plain speech has arrived. Parrhēsía — speaking openly, without concealment or circumlocution — is the opposite of paroimía. Their confidence is premature; v31 will test its depth.
30: νῦν οἴδαμεν ὅτι οἶδας πάντα — "now we know that you know all things." The disciples are stating what has been implicit throughout the Gospel: Jesus's supernatural knowledge of human hearts (John 1:47–48; John 4:17–18; John 6:64; John 11:14). καὶ οὐ χρείαν ἔχεις ἵνα τίς σε ἐρωτᾷ — "and you have no need that anyone question you." They recognize that Jesus answered their unvoiced question (v19) — he knew what they wanted to ask before they asked. ἐν τούτῳ πιστεύομεν ὅτι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ἐξῆλθες — "by this we believe that you came from God." The aorist exēlthes echoes v28's exēlthon: the disciples name what Jesus just stated about himself, now confessing it as their own belief.
31: ἄρτι πιστεύετε — "do you now believe?" The word árti (G737) — now, at this moment — carries a tone of gentle irony in context. Jesus accepts neither confirms nor dismisses their confession, but poses the question that exposes the fragility of their moment-of-clarity faith. The next verse (v32) immediately puts the "now" of their belief against the "coming hour" of their scattering.
32: ἰδοὺ ἔρχεται ὥρα καὶ ἐλήλυθεν — "behold, an hour is coming and has come." The combination of future (érchetai) and perfect (elēlythen) in the same clause is striking — the hour is both imminent and already present. ἵνα σκορπισθῆτε ἕκαστος εἰς τὰ ἴδια — "that you will be scattered, each to his own." Skorpísthēte (G4650, aorist passive subjunctive) — to scatter, to disperse. The Evangelist uses the language of Zechariah 13:7 ("strike the shepherd and the sheep will be scattered"), cited explicitly in Mark 14:27. κἀμὲ μόνον ἀφῆτε — "and you will leave me alone." Aphēte (aorist subjunctive of aphíēmi) — the same verb as "leave" in John 14:27 ("I leave you peace") and John 16:28 ("I leave the world"). The disciples will leave Jesus; Jesus leaves them peace. The contrast is deliberate. καὶ οὐκ εἰμὶ μόνος, ὅτι ὁ πατὴρ μετ᾽ ἐμοῦ ἐστιν — "and I am not alone, because the Father is with me." The disciples' abandonment does not produce aloneness; the Father's presence is constant.
33: ταῦτα λελάληκα ὑμῖν ἵνα ἐν ἐμοὶ εἰρήνην ἔχητε — "these things I have spoken to you so that in me you may have peace." The final summary of the entire Farewell Discourse (John 14–16). The perfect lelalēka — completed, standing disclosure. The peace is located en emoi — "in me" — not in changed circumstances, not in the absence of trouble. ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ θλῖψιν ἔχετε — "in the world you have tribulation." Present indicative — a fact of existence. Thlípsis (G2347) — pressure, compression, tribulation. ἀλλὰ θαρσεῖτε, ἐγὼ νενίκηκα τὸν κόσμον — "but take courage — I have overcome the world." Tharséō (G2293, present imperative) — be of good courage, take heart. Neníkēka — perfect: "I have overcome and the overcoming stands." The perfect is the foundation of the imperative: take courage because the victory is already accomplished, already fixed in the past and present simultaneously. The cosmic confrontation Jesus announced (John 12:31, John 14:30) has its verdict: the ruler of the world is judged, the world is overcome, and the overcoming is complete.
